children.html: MsM_sMs​

children.value: is measured on Rayleigh waves; dispersion and excitation efficiency (particularly the depth dependence) directly affect surface-wave magnitude estimates.
Earthquake magnitude is defined through an empirical regression that maps a seismogram measurement to event size. The general form shared by most magnitude scales is:

children.html: M=log⁡10(measurement)+F(Δ,h)+S+CM = \log_{10}(\text{measurement}) + F(\Delta, h) + S + CM=log10​(measurement)+F(Δ,h)+S+C
F(Δ,h)F(\Delta, h)F(Δ,h)

children.value: that accounts for geometrical spreading and anelastic attenuation along the propagation path

children.html: SSS

children.value: that absorbs local amplification or deamplification effects (often implicit or averaged across the network)

children.html: CCC

children.value: that anchors the scale to a chosen normalization
“measurement” denotes the seismogram observable: peak amplitude, amplitude-to-period ratio

children.html: A/TA/TA/T

children.value: The logarithmic form reflects the enormous dynamic range of earthquake ground motions — amplitudes span many orders of magnitude across event sizes.

A magnitude value is meaningful only in conjunction with (i) the observable that was measured and (ii) the calibration (regression coefficients, distance corrections, station terms) that was applied. Different networks can report different values for the same nominal magnitude type because they adopt different correction functions and regression constants.

children.html: MLM_LML​
MLM_LML​

children.value: in 1935 as the first quantitative earthquake magnitude scale. The original definition was tied to a specific instrument — the

— with a natural period of approximately 0.8 s and a peak response near 1 Hz. Richter defined magnitude zero as the event that would produce a peak trace amplitude of 1 μm on a Wood–Anderson instrument at an epicentral distance of 100 km in southern California.
The local magnitude is defined as:

children.html: ML=log⁡10(A)−log⁡10 ⁣(A0(Δ))M_L = \log_{10}(A) - \log_{10}\!\big(A_0(\Delta)\big)ML​=log10​(A)−log10​(A0​(Δ))
AAA

children.value: measured on a Wood–Anderson seismometer (or a simulated Wood–Anderson response), typically the maximum of the two horizontal components

— the original Richter curve for southern California does not apply universally.

children.label: 5. Duration and coda magnitude M_d / M_c

children.value: Duration and coda magnitudes estimate event size from the
temporal extent of the seismogram signal

children.html: Md=alog⁡10(tcoda)+b Δ+cM_d = a \log_{10}(t_{\text{coda}}) + b\,\Delta + cMd​=alog10​(tcoda​)+bΔ+c
tcodat_{\text{coda}}tcoda​

children.value: — the elapsed time from the origin (or first arrival) until the signal envelope decays below a prescribed threshold relative to the ambient noise level

children.html: aaa
bbb
ccc

children.value: are empirically determined regression coefficients, calibrated regionally
of body waves by small-scale heterogeneities in the crust and upper mantle. The coda envelope decays approximately as a power law in time, modulated by the

children.html: QcQ_cQc​

children.value: that characterizes intrinsic and scattering attenuation. Larger earthquakes produce stronger initial radiation and therefore longer-duration coda above any fixed threshold — hence the correlation between

children.html: tcodat_{\text{coda}}tcoda​
MdM_dMd​
McM_cMc​

children.value: monitoring small-to-moderate seismicity, for several reasons:
Duration measurements are less sensitive to instrument calibration errors than peak amplitudes.
They do not require precise knowledge of the instrument response, making the method robust for heterogeneous station types.
Clipped waveforms (common for nearby events on high-gain instruments) do not preclude a valid duration measurement.

: elevated background noise shortens the apparent coda duration and biases the magnitude downward. The precise definition of “coda end” (threshold criterion, smoothing window) must be standardized within a network for consistent results.

: beyond a certain event size, the measured amplitude in a fixed frequency band ceases to increase proportionally with the true source strength. The magnitude estimate levels off even as

children.value: at large magnitudes are not errors — they reflect the band-limited nature of the measurement and carry information about the source spectrum shape.
When different agencies report different magnitude values for the same event (even for the same nominal scale), the discrepancy typically arises from one or more of the following:

children.html: F(Δ,h)F(\Delta, h)F(Δ,h)

children.value: calibrated with different datasets and regional attenuation models
, or the absence of site corrections entirely

children.html: MLM_LML​

children.value: , which historically required a Wood–Anderson instrument)
used to determine the calibration coefficients
These are not pathologies but inherent features of empirically calibrated scales. Magnitude values should always be interpreted with reference to the issuing agency and its calibration.

children.value: ). For seismic hazard analysis and scaling-law studies, a uniform magnitude measure is required. Agencies construct

children.html: MwM_wMw​

children.value: . These conversions must account for saturation — a linear regression of

children.html: mbm_bmb​
MwM_wMw​

children.value: is inappropriate if the data span the saturation regime. Studies such as Ristau (2009) and Dost et al. (2018) illustrate the care required in regional magnitude calibration and the systematic biases that arise from network-specific choices.
Earthquake early warning systems require magnitude estimates within seconds of the first P-wave detection. These rapid estimates are inherently

(only the initial portion of the rupture has been recorded) and may saturate for large events whose rupture duration exceeds the available data window. Robust algorithms must detect and flag the saturation regime to avoid underestimating hazard.
Comparing magnitudes measured at different frequencies provides a window into the

11. What we deliberately did not cover

children.label: 11. What we deliberately did not cover

children.value: Full spectral fitting and stress drop estimation.
Extracting corner frequency and stress drop from spectral ratios requires detailed modeling beyond the scope of this lecture.
The joint inversion of distance correction curves and station terms (e.g., Bakun & Joyner, 1984) is an important operational topic but involves regression methodology beyond what we develop here.

children.html: MeM_eMe​

children.value: The energy magnitude, based on radiated seismic energy rather than moment, provides complementary information about source dynamics but requires broadband energy integration.

children.html: McM_cMc​

children.value: Statistical analysis of catalog completeness — determining the minimum magnitude above which all events are reliably detected — is a separate topic in seismicity analysis.
The iterative refinement of distance corrections and regression coefficients across agencies and decades is a rich topic in the history of seismology.

amplitudes on real and synthetic waveforms and compute toy
